nativeRange.habitat: The Green Neon Tetra is native to slow-moving blackwater streams and flooded forest tributaries of the upper Rio Negro and upper Orinoco basins. The water in these habitats is tannin-stained, very soft, and highly acidic, with sandy substrate, accumulations of leaf litter, and dense riparian vegetation.

diet.notes: The Green Neon Tetra is a micro omnivore that tends to accept small prepared foods, including crumbled flakes, nano pellets, micro pellets, and freeze-dried tubifex. Live and frozen micro foods — baby brine shrimp, cyclops, daphnia, and bloodworm — are generally relished and are recommended for maintaining condition and colour.

avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them

imageCandidates.notes: Christian Menke; 1600x1385. School of multiple green neon tetras showing the full-length iridescent blue-green stripe and dull red confined to the caudal region; best represents this schooling species. Slight in-water particle haze but individuals are crisp.

Sascha Biedermann; 1456x1079. Tack-sharp single specimen, whole fish in frame, clearly showing the full-length blue-green stripe running nose to tail with dull posterior red - strong diagnostic clarity.

Cangadoba; 3840x2160. Densely planted blackwater-style aquascape with a large school of green neons, showing typical habitat and shoaling behaviour; individual fish are small/distant so kept as context filler.

summary: The Green Neon Tetra (Paracheirodon simulans) is the smallest of the three Paracheirodon species, distinguished by a vivid iridescent blue-green stripe and a muted red patch confined to the tail. It tends to be more demanding than its relatives, requiring soft, acidic blackwater conditions and a mature, stable aquarium.
careNotes:
The Green Neon Tetra is the smallest of the three Paracheirodon species and tends to be the most demanding in terms of water chemistry. It generally does best in mature, biologically established aquariums with stable, soft, acidic water closely resembling its blackwater origins. Water parameter swings are reported to be particularly stressful for this species, especially for wild-caught specimens, which make up the majority of trade stock — captive-bred individuals are available from some specialty suppliers but remain uncommon in general retail.

Water chemistry is critical: pH is generally advised in the 4.0–6.5 range, with hardness ideally at or near zero (gH 0–4, kH 0–2). This is notably stricter than both the Neon Tetra (Paracheirodon innesi) and Cardinal Tetra (Paracheirodon axelrodi), which tolerate somewhat harder and more neutral water.

The species is noticeably smaller and paler than its congeners. The brilliant blue-green iridescent stripe runs the length of the body; the red stripe is restricted to the lower caudal region and is considerably duller than in the Cardinal Tetra or Neon Tetra. All three species (P. simulans, P. innesi, P. axelrodi) constitute the entire genus Paracheirodon.

A school of at least 8 individuals is recommended; 10–15 or more in larger setups is preferable. Dim lighting, dark substrate, driftwood, and soft leaf litter will help them display their best colouration and reduce stress. Gentle filtration is important — the species is native to slow-flowing waters and does not tolerate strong currents well.

Some taxonomic authorities place the species in the family Acestrorhamphidae following a recent reclassification, though most aquarium hobby sources continue to treat it as a member of Characidae.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been documented but is generally considered difficult and is rarely achieved in the hobby. The majority of trade specimens are wild-caught from the Rio Negro and adjacent blackwater tributaries; captive-bred individuals are available from some specialty suppliers but remain uncommon in the broader trade. Breeding appears to require very soft, acidic water (pH around 5.0–6.0, near-zero hardness), dim or blackout conditions, and has been reported to occur nocturnally. Eggs are thought to be light-sensitive. Spawning in schools rather than isolated pairs has been noted to improve success rates. Fry require infusoria and micro foods at first feeding.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source; adult size, temperature (74–82°F / 23–28°C), pH (3.0–6.5 natural; up to 7.0–8.0 in captivity), tank size (10 gal minimum), swim zone, diet, temperament, grouping, and native range. Tank size recommendation biased toward 60 L per playbook guidance for proper schooling group of 8+.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Characidae/Characiformes), size (15–25 mm), pH (3.0–6.5), hardness (18–143 ppm ≈ 1–8 dGH; lower end strongly preferred), tank dimensions (45×30 cm base ≈ 60 L equivalent), gregarious schooling behaviour. Temperature listed as 21–35°C including natural river extremes; aquarium range narrowed to 23–28°C per other sources. Wild-caught sensitivity and breeding rarity confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (2.0 cm SL), temperature (23–27°C), pH (5.5–6.0), hardness (up to 4 dH), native range (upper Negro and Orinoco basins). FishBase classifies family as Acestrorhamphidae; Characidae retained per hobby consensus. pH range averaged across all sources to 4.0–6.5 for aquarium recommendation.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (~2.5 cm), temperature (75–85°F / 24–29°C), pH (5.0–6.5), native range (Orinoco and Negro Rivers), lifespan (2–3 years captivity), and minimum group size (6–8). Lifespan averaged with cardinal tetra data to give 2–5 year range. Venezuela confirmed in native range.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely achieved in the hobby and is generally considered difficult. Most trade stock is wild-caught, with captive-bred specimens available from some specialty suppliers but uncommon in general retail. Successful spawning has been reported to require soft, acidic blackwater conditions, dim or blackout lighting, and appears to occur nocturnally. Fry require infusoria and other micro foods at first feeding.
